coredata.dc:title: Optimization of electrical performance and stability of fully solution-driven α-InGaZnO thin-film transistors by graphene quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.prism:publisher: Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Xiaofen
He, Gang
Wang, Leini
Wang, Wenhao
Jiang, Shanshan
Fang, Zebo
coredata.dc:description: This work presents solution-processed high-performance graphene quantum dots (GQDs) decorated amorphous InGaZnO (α-IGZO) thin-film transistors (TFTs) based on ZrO x as gate dielectrics. Compare with pure IGZO TFTs, GQDs-modified α-IGZO TFTs devices with optimized doping content have demonstrated better performances, including a larger field-effect mobility (μ FE) of 35.91 cm2 V–1 s–1, a higher on/off current ratio (I ON/I OFF) of 5.04 × 108, a smaller subthreshold swing (SS) of 0.11 V dec–1 and a smaller interfacial trap states (D it, 1.57 × 1012 cm−2). Moreover, the GQDs-doped IGZO TFTs with a doping concentration of 0.5 mg ml–1 have shown excellent stability under bias stress and illumination stress conditions. To demonstrate the potential applications of α-IGZO TFTs in logic circuits, a resistor-loaded unipolar inverter based on GQDs-IGZO/ZrO x has been integrated, demonstrating good dynamic behavior and a high gain of 9.3. Low-frequency noise (LFN) characteristics of GQDs-IGZO/ZrO x TFTs have suggested that the fluctuations in mobility are the noise source. Based on all the experimental findings, it can be concluded that solution-processed GQDs-IGZO/ZrO x TFT may envision promising applications in optoelectronics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thin-film transistor
α-InGaZnO
Graphene quantum dots
Stability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of GQDS-IGZO TFTs and characterization

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the IGZO TFTs with (a) undoping and (b) GQDs-doping channel layer.
AFM images of (a) GQDs-IGZO-0 thin film, (b) GQDs-IGZO-1 thin film, (c) GQDs-IGZO-2 thin film, and (d) GQDs-IGZO-3 thin film.
(a) Optical transmittance, (b) the determination of band gap, (c) thermal behavior, and (d) XRD patterns of IGZO thin films with different GQDs concentrations.
XPS spectra of (a) C 1 s and (c) O 1 s. Semiquantitative analyses of carbon-related (b) and oxygen-related (d) components variation for GQDs-IGZO films with different doping concentrations.

Output curve of (a) GQDs-IGZO-0 TFT, (b) GQDs-IGZO-1 TFT, (c) GQDs-IGZO-2 TFT, and (d) GQDs-IGZO-3 TFT. (e) Transfer characteristics of the GQDs-IGZO TFTs. (f) Electrical parameters of GQDs-IGZO TFTs under different GQDs-doping concentrations.

Variations of transfer curves for (a) GQDs-IGZO-0 TFT and (b) GQDs-IGZO-2 TFT under PBS test, (c) GQDs-IGZO-0 TFT and (d) GQDs-IGZO-2 TFT under NBS test. Mechanisms of the bias instability for GQDs-IGZO TFT under (e) PBS and (f) NBS.
Transfer curves under PBIS test of (a–c) GQDs-IGZO-0 TFT and (d–f) GQDs-IGZO-2 TFT under red, green, and blue light illumination.
Transfer curves under NBIS test of (a–c) GQDs-IGZO-0 TFT and (d–f) GQDs-IGZO-2 TFT under red, green, and blue light illumination.
V TH shifts as a function of stress time for GQDs-IGZO TFT under (a) darkness, (b) red, (c) green, and (d) blue light illumination.

Schematic diagram of (a) photo-induced ionization of Vo-associated trap states for GQDs-IGZO TFT. The accumulation of fixed electronics in the gate dielectric under light illumination for (b) PBIS and (c) NBIS.

(a) The resistor-loaded inverter based on GQDs-IGZO-2 TFT. (b) VTC curves of GQDs-IGZO-2 TFT. (c) The voltage gain of the inverter GQDs-IGZO-2 TFT at different applied voltages. (d) Dynamic response behavior curves.
Normalized S ID/I D 2 with (a, b) a function of frequency and (c) different V GS–V TH of the GQDs-IGZO TFTs.

head.ce:title: Optimization of electrical performance and stability of fully solution-driven α-InGaZnO thin-film transistors by graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Materials Science and Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Integration Circuits, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of Integration Circuits, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mathematical Information, Shaoxing University, Shaoxing 312000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cSchool of Mathematical Information, Shaoxing University, Shaoxing 312000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work presents solution-processed high-performance graphene quantum dots (GQDs) decorated amorphous InGaZnO (α-IGZO) thin-film transistors (TFTs) based on ZrO x as gate dielectrics. Compare with pure IGZO TFTs, GQDs-modified α-IGZO TFTs devices with optimized doping content have demonstrated better performances, including a larger field-effect mobility (μ FE) of 35.91 cm2 V–1 s–1, a higher on/off current ratio (I ON/I OFF) of 5.04 × 108, a smaller subthreshold swing (SS) of 0.11 V dec–1 and a smaller interfacial trap states (D it, 1.57 × 1012 cm−2). Moreover, the GQDs-doped IGZO TFTs with a doping concentration of 0.5 mg ml–1 have shown excellent stability under bias stress and illumination stress conditions. To demonstrate the potential applications of α-IGZO TFTs in logic circuits, a resistor-loaded unipolar inverter based on GQDs-IGZO/ZrO x has been integrated, demonstrating good dynamic behavior and a high gain of 9.3. Low-frequency noise (LFN) characteristics of GQDs-IGZO/ZrO x TFTs have suggested that the fluctuations in mobility are the noise source. Based on all the experimental findings, it can be concluded that solution-processed GQDs-IGZO/ZrO x TFT may envision promising applications in optoelectronics.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, amorphous InGaZnO (α-IGZO) thin film transistors (TFTs) have extensively been paid more attention due to their superior performance such as high mobility, high optical transparency, and high stability [1–4]. In the amorphous state of IGZO, In ions contribute to the increase of electron mobility and the open-state current of the device, Ga ions suppress the generation of oxygen vacancies and reduce material defects, thus controlling the carrier concentration, the stable tetrahedral structure of Zn ions promotes and maintains the amorphous structure of IGZO and enhances the stability of the device. When α-IGZO TFTs are used to drive the pixels in a liquid crystal display (LCD) panel, the TFTs are subjected to backlighting. An important advantage of α-IGZO TFTs for panel display technology is that the IGZO material is a wide-band semiconductor through which visible light can pass, thus increasing the brightness of the screen. Therefore, when IGZO TFTs are used in LCD panels or bottom-illuminated active-matrix organic light-emitting diode (AMOLED) panels, light stability is of great importance [5,6]. It has been demonstrated that doping the IGZO with some atoms such as N, Mg, and nanometer dots can improve the electrical characteristics and light illumination stability of the TFTs devices [7–9].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have a non-zero bandgap and luminesce on excitation [10]. Chemically derived GQDs have shown very broad emission line widths due to many kinds of chemical bonding with different energy levels, which significantly degrades color purity and color tunability [11]. GQDs have excellent light fastness, good biocompatibility, good tunable photoluminescence (PL) properties, peculiar up-conversion properties, excellent catalytic activity, chemical inertness, etc. [12–14]. The electronic transition between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) directly generates UV/visible light absorption and PL, and this edge structure is the main cause of the intense luminescence induced in GQDs [15]. Edge effects and quantum binding give GQDs great potential for applications in next-generation electronic and optoelectronic devices [16,17].

However, few reports have been focused on the effect of GQDs doping on the electrical properties as well as the stability of IGZO TFTs. In this work, the sol-gel method with spin-coating was carried out to synthesize GQDs-IGZO thin films. Changes in the microstructure and composition of GQDs-IGZO films with different doping levels were systematically investigated. In addition, the bias and illumination stress stability for GQDs-IGZO/ZrO x TFTs have been investigated and the relationship between incident light energy and photo-induced instability has been explored. To demonstrate the potential possibility of GQDs-IGZO thin films in logic circuits, their applications in GQD-IGZO TFTs and resistor-loaded inverters based on solution-processed ZrO x gate dielectrics were also demonstrated, and then evaluated carefully. Based on the analyses from low-frequency noise (LFN) characteristics of GQDs-IGZO/ZrO x TFTs, the noise source contributing to the mobility fluctuations has been determined. The improvement in the performance of IGZO-based TFTs by GQDs doping engineering has indicated that fully solution-driven α-InGaZnO TFTs have promising applications in flat panel displays and logic circuits.

The 0.1 M ZrO x solution was obtained by dissolving 322.3 mg zirconium chloride octahydrate (ZrOCl2·8H2O, ≥ 99%) in 10 mL 2-methoxyethanol (2-ME). The IGZO precursor solution with concentrations of indium, gallium, and zinc precursors was controlled at 0.08, 0.01, and 0.02 M, respectively. And the solution was prepared by mixing 240.8 mg Indium nitrate hydrate (In(NO3)3·xH2O, ≥ 99%), 25.6 mg Gallium nitrate hydrate (Ga(NO3)3·xH2O, ≥ 99%), and 59.4 mg Zinc nitrate hydrate (Zn(NO3)3·xH2O, ≥ 99%) in 10 ml 2-ME. At the same time, the IGZO precursor solution was added to different levels of GQDs (0, 2, 5, and 8 mg), stirred for 6 h, and aged for 24 h before preparation for use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of GQDS-IGZO TFTs and characterization
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 , the schematic diagram of the IGZO-based TFTs with the bottom-gate top-contact structure on the P++ Si substrate has been displayed. Firstly, the ZrO x precursor solution was spun three times on the P++ Si substrate at 5000 rpm for 15 s. Plasma washing for 5 min before each spin coating and baking at 200 °C for 10 min after spin coating. After 30 min of UV treatment, the deposited films were annealed at 600 °C for 2 h. Secondly, IGZO-based TFTs were prepared by spin-coating IGZO and GQDs-IGZO precursor solution on ZrO x dielectrics at 3000 rpm for 15 s. The samples were baked at 150 °C for a few minutes and then annealed in air at 300 °C for 1 h. Finally, Al film was deposited by thermal evaporation using a shadow mask with a width of 1000 µm and a length of 200 µm to form the source and the drain electrode. All the corresponding samples were named GQDs-IGZO-0, GQDs-IGZO-1, GQDs-IGZO-2, and GQDs-IGZO-3 with GQDs concentrations of 0, 0.2, 0.5, and 0.8 mg ml–1, respectively.

The optical properties, surface morphologies, crystal structures, and chemical bonding states of the pure IGZO and GQDs-doped IGZO thin films were carried out by using UV visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis-2550, Shimadzu), atomic force microscopy (AFM, 5500 M, Hitachi, Japan), X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Empyrean S3, Netherlands) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), respectively. The thermal behavior of the precursor solution was measured by a thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA, STA449F3). The electrical performances and resistor-loaded unipolar inverter of the TFTs were investigated by a semiconductor parameter analyzer (Agilent B1500A, Keithley E4990A, and Keithley 2636B). The LFN properties of the GQDs-IGZO TFTs were measured by a semiconductor parameter analyzer (PDA FS-380). In addition, the electrical parameters are derived from the following formula:
(1)
I DS
=
W
2 L
C i
μ FE
(
V GS
−
V TH
)
2
(2)
SS =
d
V GS
d
(
log
V DS
)
(3)
D it
=
[
SS · log
( e )
k T / q
− 1
]
C i
q
Where W is the channel width (1000 µm), L is the channel length (200 µm) in the shadow mask, C i is the areal capacitance of the gate dielectric, V GS is the source-gate voltage, V TH is the threshold voltage, I DS is the saturation current, SS is the subthreshold swing, μ FE is the field-effect mobility and D it is the interface state density. k represents Boltzmann's constant and T represents the absolute temperature.

As shown in Fig. 2 , AFM measurement was carried out to investigate the surface morphology of GQDs-IGZO thin films. The root mean square (RMS) of IGZO-based thin films annealed at 300 °C has been determined to be 0.249, 0.239, 0.207, and 0.233 nm for the GQDs-IGZO-0, GQDs-IGZO-1, GQDs-IGZO-2, and GQDs-IGZO-3, respectively. It is obvious that the surface morphology of all GQDS-IGZO films is very smooth and uniform. The RMS first decreases and then increases with the GQDs doping increase, and the GQDs-IGZO-2 has a minimum value. We can hypothesize that a moderate amount of GQDs can fill the large number of nanopores produced during the annealing process and thus reduce the surface roughness of IGZO thin films. However, with the continued increase in the GQDs doping content, there will be a pile-up and the increased surface roughness will be detected. As a result, it can be assumed that GQDs-IGZO thin films can act as the channel layer in TFTs and small RMS is good for obtaining high-stability TFTs [18].

The spin-coating-derived pure IGZO and GQDs-doped IGZO films were prepared on the quartz substrates and the optical properties of samples were obtained using UV–Vis. As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the transmittance values of all the prepared films in the visible region are high (above 88%). It is quite clear that GQDs-IGZO samples have more excellent optical transmittance, demonstrating its feasibility in transparent electronic devices. In addition, the transmittance of the GQDs-IGZO samples increases slightly as the GQDs concentration rises, which can be attributed to the strong photoluminescence of GQDs [19]. The absorption edge shifts towards a shorter wavelength region with increasing the GQDs concentration, revealing that the band gap of IGZO can be influenced by the doped GQDs content. As demonstrated in Fig. 3(b), it can be seen that the bandgap of GQDs-IGZO thin films increases from 4.25 eV for the GQDs-IGZO-0 sample to 4.68 eV for GQDs-IGZO-3, respectively. As reported, the blue shift in the band gap can be attributed to the reduction in oxygen vacancies. The reason for the narrowing of the band gap is that the excess oxygen vacancies act as shallow donors, providing the conduction electrons [20,21]. It is clear that the increased band gap with increasing the GQDs content can originate from the reduced oxygen vacancies, which will be discussed later. In addition, the other reason is that GQDs have a bund gap due to their quantum confinement [22]. In short, GQDs doping can improve the transmittance and band gap of IGZO thin films.

Fig. 3(c) shows the thermal behavior of GQDS-IGZO precursor powder with three intervals of weight loss. The first step (below 150 °C) is due to the presence of residual solvent in the precursor powder. The second step (between 150 to 320 °C) can be attributed to the decomposition of –(NO3)3 to –(OH)3. The reason for the third step (between 320 and 400 °C) is the thermal decomposition and dehydroxylation of –OOH and the formation of IGZO. After 400 °C, the weight loss does not change, indicating the formation of IGZO film [23]. The transfer temperature of the GQDs-doped IGZO films is lower than that of the undoped IGZO films, indicating that the doping of GQDs can reduce the preparation temperature of the IGZO films. This is because graphene-like materials exhibit rapid thermal expansion with the intense gas release [24]. As shown in Fig. 3(d), XRD patterns of GQDs-IGZO thin films have shown that no characteristic peaks are observed, indicating the formation of the amorphous state of all the films, regardless of the GQDs doping content. As we all know, amorphous films play an important role in improving the performance of TFT devices, because they have many advantages such as uniform area, smooth surface, good stability, and low density of interfacial states [25].

As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectra of the GQDs can be deconvoluted into three peaks corresponding to the C–C bond in aromatic rings (284.8 eV), C–O bond (286.4 eV), and C=O bond (288.6 eV) [26]. What's interesting about the results is that the percentages of C–C, C–O, and C=O remain constant respectively with the increase of the GQDs concentration. All the GQDs-IGZO films have high contents of C–C (92%), and the same contents of C–O and C=O (4%) (Fig. 4(b)). From this, it can be seen that there is no change in C 1 s as GQDs concentration increases. The O 1 s spectra of the GQDs-IGZO samples were fine-scanned and each peak was deconvoluted into three subpeaks (Fig. 4(c)). The three peaks located at 529.6, 530.9, and 531.5 eV can be attributed to oxide lattice (M–O), oxygen vacancies (Vo), and hydroxyl species (M–OH) of IGZO-based films, respectively [18]. The content of the specific O types of the GQDs-IGZO samples was quantitatively shown in Fig. 4(d). It can be seen that with the increasing of GQDs concentration, the contents of M–O retain continuous increasing from 46% to 51%. Vo composition first stays at 28% and then decreases to 25%. The M–OH species first decrease from 27% to 25%, then increases to 26% and remain constant with GQDs-IGZO-3. The lower ratio of Vo is related to the charge trapping, which gives proof of the blue-shift phenomenon for the absorption edge. In addition, the lower Vo contributes to the increased electrical stability of the TFT device [8]. Combined with the changes in C 1 s and O 1 s, one can speculate that GQDs doping has no effect on C 1 s, while it possibly has a role in reducing Vo. Vo is the most important factor to affect the electrical performance of TFTs and it can be modulated by GQDs doping engineering.

To investigate the effect of GQDs on the electrical properties of IGZO-based TFTs, electrical tests were carried out on GQDs-IGZO/ZrO x TFTs. Fig. 5 (a–d) shows the output characteristic curves of the GQDs-IGZO/ZrO x TFTs at various gate voltages from 0 to 2 V with the step of 0.5 V. It can be seen that all the TFT devices showed typical n-channel conduction behavior with obvious current saturation and pinch-off voltage. Based on Fig. 5, it can be noted that the saturation current is 11.05, 12.55, 16.55, and 8.70 µA at 2.0 V for GQDs-IGZO-0, GQDs-IGZO-1, GQDs-IGZO-2, and GQDs-IGZO-3, respectively. It is obvious that the saturation current first increases and then decreases with the increase of GQDs concentration. In addition, the output curves increase linearly for all the IGZO-based TFTs suggest that excellent ohmic contact between GQDs-IGZO thin films and Al electrodes [27]. Compared with GQDS-IGZO-0 TFT, the higher saturation current in GQDS-IGZO-2 TFT indicates the higher on-current level of GQDs doped IGZO TFTs. We expect them to exhibit stable unipolar switching characteristics as they have fairly high on-state current characteristics for IGZO TFTs embedded by GQDs. The transfer characteristic curves of all the produced GQDs-IGZO TFTs are shown in Fig. 5(e). Meanwhile, the extracted electrical parameters of GQDs-IGZO TFTs are summarized in Fig. 5(f) and Table 1 . It can be noted that with the increase in the GQDs concentration, µ FE and a higher on/off current ratio (I ON/I OFF) firstly increase and then decrease. In the meanwhile, SS and D it have shown the opposite trend. It is quite clear that GQDs-IGZO-2 TFT has exhibited superior electrical performance, including a μ FE of 35.91 cm2 V–1 s–1, an I ON/I OFF of 5.04 × 108, and a SS of 0.11 V dec–1. It can be clearly seen that compared with GQDs-IGZO-0 TFT, the μ FE of GQDs-IGZO-2 TFTs increases obviously. As reported, the incorporation of C can significantly increase the carrier concentration of oxide semiconductors [28]. GQDs have a great influence on the value of μ FE for the reason that the carrier mobilities of GQDs-doped IGZO TFTs can be increased due to the high electron mobility of graphene, indicating faster switching speeds and higher operating [29,30]. Besides, IGZO TFTs doped with GQDs have a higher I ON/I OFF, which can be the due to the smaller I OFF. As the doping of GQDs increases, the concentration of oxygen vacancies and free carriers decreases, which leads to reduced I OFF and increased I ON/I OFF. However, continuing to increase the doping concentration of GQDs leads to a decrease in device performance, which can be attributed to the increase in the trap state near the GQDs-IGZO/ZrO x TFTs interface. The value of SS reflects the speed at which the device switches from off to on, and a smaller SS means that the device switches from off to on more quickly. Graphene plays an important role in the field of high-speed electronics because of its high current density and high saturation rate [31]. SS can reflect the value of these defects because as gate voltage (V G) increases, the concentration of carriers induced by the electric field increases, and some of these carriers are used to fill the defects, which results in a macroscopic increase in SS. Based on the SS values, D it values for IGZO-based TFTs doped with different GQDs concentrations can be obtained. As shown in Table 1 and Fig. 5(f), it can be noted that compared to GQDs-IGZO-0 TFTs, GQDs-doped IGZO TFTs have reduced D it values and GQDs-IGZO-2 TFTs have the minimal values, indicating that GQDs doping contributes to the reduction of defect states and the formation of a high-quality GQDs-IGZO/ZrO x interface. The small values of D it and SS not only improve the stability of the device but also facilitate carrier transmission on the interface for the reason that a high-quality interface is formed. Continued doping of GQDs in IGZO leads to a decrease in TFT performance parameters due to the introduction of a lot of oxygen-containing groups by the GQDs, which will act as a carrier for trap states and trap movements. There is no conductive channel in the device when no gate voltage is applied, and the TFT forms a conductive channel when the gate voltage increases to a certain value, which is also known as V TH. The GQDs-IGZO-2 TFT has a higher V TH compared with the GQDs-IGZO-0 TFT, which means that the higher of the operating voltage for the TFT and the higher of the power consumption for the device. The doping of GQDs leads to a positive V TH, which is due to the fact that the GQDs compensate for the excess carriers in the IGZO, and the V TH shifts towards enhancement mode (E-mode) device characteristics. The flexibility of the graphene structure contributes to the excellent electrical properties of the GQDs-doped IGZO TFTs [32]. In summary, these properties of a higher µ FE, a larger I ON/I OFF, a smaller SS, and a D it value for GQDs-IGZO-2 TFT, provide a firm foundation for improving TFT properties and latent applications in e-book and TFT-LCD.

As the basic unit of an integrated circuit, the stability of the TFT is very important. Normally, the stability of TFT is determined by the V TH, which affects the display device brightness. To prove the effects of GQDs doping on the stability of IGZO-based TFTs, the positive bias stress (PBS) and negative bias stress (NBS) stability of GQDs-IGZO-0 and GQDs-IGZO-2 TFTs are measured under a constant gate bias voltage of 2.0 V and source and drain grounded. The transfer curves of GQDs-IGZO-0 and GQDs-IGZO-2 TFTs shift to the right side for 0.92 V (Fig. 6 (a)) and 0.14 V (Fig. 6(b)) during PBS duration. It can be noted that the GQDs-IGZO-2 TFTs exhibit better PBS stability with a smaller ΔV TH compared to the GQDs-IGZO-0 TFTs, illustrating that more electrons are being captured near/at the IGZO interface and dielectric for GQDs-IGZO-0 TFTs, whereas proper doping of GQDs suppresses the trapped charge in the film because of the lower interfacial state at the dielectric and channel interface for GQDs-IGZO-2 TFTs, which is in good agreement with the previous D it characterization. Meanwhile, the transfer curves shift to the left side by 0.91 V (Fig. 6(c)) and 0.05 V (Fig. 6(d)) for GQDs-IGZO-0 and GQDs-IGZO-2 TFT during NBS duration. The leftward shift in the transfer curve may be due to the release of electrons from oxygen defects in the active layer under continuous gate bias stress. In conclusion, the stability of GQDs-IGZO-2 TFT has been improved greatly compared with GQDs-IGZO-0 TFT. These results suggest that appropriate GQDs can attenuate charge capture in the dielectric region or at the IGZO/ZrO x interface.

In order to further explain the mechanism for the shift in the V TH under PBS and NBS of the GQDs-IGZO TFTs, a mechanism of the energy bands is shown in Fig. 6(e, f), which E c, E f, E i, and E v represent for the conduction band, fermi energy level, intermediate energy band, and valence band, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6(e), when performing PBS measurements for the device, the energy band bends downwards because the electrons accumulate near the interface of GQDs-IGZO and ZrO x under positive gate voltage. Besides, the positive shifts phenomenon occurs because the adsorption of O2 molecules which act as acceptors can also capture electrons by the equation O2 (gas) + e–→ O2 – (solid). As a result, electron carriers can be consumed by the adsorption of O2 in the channel layers, while the conduction electrons in the GQDs-IGZO channels are easily repelled by the buildup of negatively space-charged O2 – [33]. In the NBS process (Fig. 6(f)), the energy band bends upwards because electrons are depleted near the interface. In the meantime, the reason for the negative shifts is that the GQDs-IGZO channel layer will get more electrons from the ZrO x gate dielectric. Due to the diffusion of a large number of water molecules in the surrounding air and the creation of a large number of free electrons. Besides, the hygroscopic mechanism is also responsible for the instability of NBS and the reaction equation H2O (gas) + h+ → H2O+ (solid) can represent this phenomenon [34].

The positive-bias illumination stress (PBIS) stability of GQDs-IGZO-0 and GQDs-IGZO-2 TFTs are shown in Fig. 7 . Three different wavelengths for illumination measurements (625, 525, and 455 nm) have been adopted in the current experiment. The transfer curves of GQDs-IGZO-0 TFT shift to the right side by 0.36 V (Fig. 7(a)), 0.16 V (Fig. 7(b)) and 0.11 V (Fig. 7(c)) for red, green and blue light illumination with the bias application time increasing, respectively. Meanwhile, the transfer curves shift to the right side by 0.15 V (Fig. 7(d)), 0.13 V (Fig. 7(e)), and 0.07 V (Fig. 7(f)) for GQDs-IGZO-2 TFT. It is obvious that the stability of GQDs-IGZO-2 TFT is improved greatly compared with GQDs-IGZO-0 TFT. Unlike red and green illumination, the increase in I OFF under blue light illumination can be attributed to two reasons. One is that the illumination excitation effect exceeds the trapping effect of the bias voltage in PBIS, which makes the electron transport in the channel layer and the device cannot be effectively switched off. The second reason is the creation of a large number of holes (h+) at the interface, which deteriorates the leakage performance of the device [35].

The negative-bias illumination stress (NBIS) stability of GQDs-IGZO-0 and GQDs-IGZO-2 TFTs are shown in Fig. 8 . The same experiment details have been carried out to obtain NBIS stability. The transfer curves of GQDs-IGZO-0 TFT shift to the left sides by 0.49 V (Fig. 8(a)), 0.15 V (Fig. 8(b)) and 0.08 V (Fig. 8(c)) for red, green, and blue illumination with increasing the bias time, respectively. However, the transfer curves shift to the left sides by 0.15 V (Fig. 8(d)), 0.14 V (Fig. 8(e)), and 0.06 V (Fig. 8(f)) for GQDs-IGZO-2 TFT. It is also obvious that the stability of GQDs-IGZO-2 TFT is improved compared with GQDs-IGZO-0 TFT. It can be noted that a lower threshold voltage shift for blue illumination has been detected compared to red and green illumination, regardless of PBIS and NBIS.

To investigate the effect of GQDs doping on the device stability, Fig. 9 summarizes the shift in V TH for GQDs-IGZO-0 and GQDs-IGZO-2 TFTs. The light illuminations with three different wavelengths (red, green, and blue) have been considered as well as the darkness. Fig. 9(a–d) shows the positive and negative shift in V TH for GQDs-IGZO-0 and GQDs-IGZO-2 TFTs under the condition of darkness, red, green, and blue light illumination, respectively. It has been found that the GQDs-IGZO-2 TFTs exhibit improved bias stress and bias illumination stress stability, indicating a much smaller threshold voltage shift than that of the GQDs-IGZO-0 TFTs. As a result, it can be inferred that GQDs-IGZO-2 TFTs demonstrate a smaller shift in V TH, indicating that the appropriate doping of GQDs into IGZO contributes to the improved stability of the devices. In order to further explain the shift in the transfer curve under PBIS and NBIS measurements, a schematic diagram is shown in Fig. 10 . As shown in Fig. 10(a), it conceptually explains the phenomenon of light-induced VO-ionization, with neutral VO forming in the deep state beyond the maximum of valence band [36]. Furthermore, the shift in V TH is closely related to the activation energy of VO-ionization and this figure makes it easier to understand how light levels affect the stability of the device. The VO transition to VO + and VO 2+ has been reported at the energies of ∼2.0 and ∼2.3 eV, respectively [37]. It is clear that the ionization for VO transition to VO + or VO 2+ was activated under the light illuminations of green and blue for it needs 2.37 and 2.73 eV to excite electrons from trap states, respectively [38,39]. It can be noted that a large number of defects are located at the front and back channels, and a large number of porosities arise from the annealing process of the films prepared by the solution method, leading to oxygen in the air environment continuing to diffuse from the active layer adsorption into the dielectric layer. At the same time, due to the depletion of electrons by oxygen and the formation of defects such as O2 – will impede the carrier transport in the channel, the positive bias will accelerate the depletion process, so the device needs a larger V TH to turn on, which exhibits a positive bias. In contrast, light releases the trapped electrons, so that the scattering effect between electrons is reduced and therefore the positive offset of the device is lower [40]. It is clear that the negative offset of V TH is due to the fact that both light-generated holes and positively charged VO can be attracted by a negative gate bias. In addition, it is possible for the light-generated holes to tunnel into the gate dielectric and thus the fixed positive charges are formed. As shown in Fig. 10(b, c), although the rate of electrons and hole injection slow down with an increase in the local built-in electric field for PBIS and NBIS. The degradation of the device does not stop due to the constant ionization of oxygen vacancies caused by light illumination.

To further explore the potential application of an inverter to verify its logical operation ability, a simple unipolar resistor-loaded inverter was integrated by combining GQDs-IGZO-2 TFT with a 10 MΩ resistor in series, as shown in Fig. 11 (a). Fig. 11(b) shows the voltage transfer characteristics (VTC) of resistor-loaded inverter inverters based on GQDs-IGZO-2 TFT at different supply voltages (V DD), where the V DD increases from 0.5 to 2.5 V and the input voltage ranges from –0.5 to 2 V. Results have shown that each characteristic curve has a high level at the output corresponding to V DD and a low level at the output which is approximately 0 V, demonstrating a good inverter performance. The gain of voltage (−∂V OUT/∂V IN) is depicted in Fig. 11(c). The maximum value of the gain observed for the inverter of GQDs-IGZO-2 TFT is 9.4 when V DD comes up to 3.0 V, which is sufficient to drive the logic components in an integrated circuit [41]. The TFT has two inputs, both the top and back gate, and the polarity of the TFT can be switched by switching the input to the back gate. The slope of the inverter transfer curve can be changed by changing the back gate voltage. Graphene, an atomically thin single sheet of graphite, is very attractive because of its excellent inherent properties, such as high carrier mobility up to 230,000 cm2 V–1 s–1 and two-dimensional geometry. The authors reportedly constructed a common-source inverter by connecting a graphene field-effect tube to a 3 kΩ off-chip load resistor. The experimental results show that graphene is very important for the common-source inverter because of the small cross-conductance, large drain conductance, and small back-gate capacitance [42]. In addition, Fig. 11(d) demonstrates that the GQDs-IGZO-2 TFT device reveals good inverse characteristics and sensitive responses to square wave signals. The superior performance of inverters shows that they possess good application prospects in logic circuits.

At last, LFN measurements were carried out for GQDs-IGZO TFTs to investigate the interface trap density and improvement in the stability of the IGZO TFTs with GQDs doping. Fig. 12 (a, b) shows the normalized drain current noise power spectral density (S ID/I D 2) with a function of frequency. The condition for the test is a fixed gate overdrive voltage (V GS–V TH) of 2.0 V together with drain-source voltage (V DS) of 2.0 V as shown in Fig. 12(a). It can be noted that the GQDs-IGZO TFTs are in good agreement with the classical 1/f noise theory and the 1/f noise curves can be acquired from the linear region of the curves [43]. Observably, the S ID/I D 2 of GQDs-IGZO TFT with GQDs doped presents a decreasing trend compared with the GQDs-IGZO-0 TFT, indicating that the interfacial trap density and the average trap density of IGZO-based TFTs reduce after doping with appropriate amounts of GQDs. The volumetric mobility fluctuation (Δμ) model proposed by Hooge and the carrier number fluctuation (ΔN) model proposed by McWhorter can be used to explain the physical mechanism of LFN [44,45]. The Δμ model has a value of –1 and the ΔN model has a value of –2 for the slope of the fitted S ID/I D 2 versus (V GS–V TH) curve [44]. As demonstrated in Fig. 12(c), the normalized S ID/I D 2 properties were estimated and analyzed under the experimental measurement parameters of V GS–V TH = (0.2–1.4) V, V DS = 2 V, and f = 20 Hz. The slope values for the GQDs-IGZO TFTs are –1.14, which is consistent with the Δμ mode. Thus it can be seen that the samples are homogeneous and the source of noise mainly results from the fluctuations in mobility [46]. Graphene devices have been reported to exhibit enhanced noise behavior, the graphene transistors with the bottom and top gate have a low 1/f noise level with the low Hooge parameter [47,48]. Combined with the electrical characteristics μ FE and D it of the GQDs-IGZO TFTs, it can be noted that the evolution pattern of the normalized drain current noise spectral density coincides with its variation trend. Hence, all the results demonstrate that the trap density can be reduced and the electrical properties can be improved with the doping of GQDs for IGZO TFTs. At the same time, the IGZO TFTs doped with GQDs have improved bias and light illumination stress stability which may expand its application in optical devices.

In summary, based on an all-solution induction route, high-performance GQDs decorated amorphous α-IGZO TFTs by using ZrO x as gate dielectrics have been integrated. Device performance analyses have confirmed that GQDs-IGZO/ZrO x TFTs with optimized doping content have demonstrated high electrical performance, including μ FE of 35.91 cm2 V–1 s–1, I ON/I OFF of 5.04 × 108, SS of 0.11 V dec–1 and D it of 1.57 × 1012 cm−2. Besides, characterization from bias and bias illumination stress stability have indicated that GQDs-IGZO-2 TFT has shown remarkable stability, which can be attributed to the reduction of oxygen vacancy defects. A resistor-loaded inverter of integrated GQDs-IGZO/ZrO x TFT demonstrates a high gain of 9.3 and good dynamic response behavior. LFN measurements have indicated that GQDs doping leads to reduced interface trap density and the noise source can originate from the fluctuations in mobility. Our strategy opens a simple and reliable path toward integrating GQDs-IGZO TFTs with low-cost and low-power consumption into future quantum dots-based optoelectronics.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11774001), the Anhui Project (No. Z010118169), and the Open Fund Project of Zhejiang Engineering Research Center of MEMS in Shaoxing University (No. MEMSZJERC2202).
